title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

texts.A: The traditional university model is undergoing a crisis of identity. For decades, a degree was a guaranteed ticket to the middle class, but as tuition fees skyrocket, the return on investment is becoming increasingly questionable. [37]While some argue that the campus experience is essential for social development, I believe that the future of learning lies in modular, online certifications that allow students to tailor their education to the specific needs of the tech-driven economy.[/37] [39]Furthermore, it is a fallacy to suggest that universities are the primary drivers of innovation; most cutting-edge research is now happening within the private sector, leaving academia struggling to remain relevant.[/39] The ivory tower is not just crumbling; it is becoming a relic of a pre-digital age.
texts.B: We must be careful not to reduce the value of a university education to a mere salary calculation. A degree provides something that a coding bootcamp or a short-term certification never can: a broad intellectual foundation and the ability to think critically across disciplines. [38]I disagree with those who claim that the rise of online learning will lead to the demise of the physical university; the networking opportunities and peer-to-peer interactions found on campus are irreplaceable.[/38] [40]That said, the current curriculum in many institutions is woefully outdated, focusing on theoretical knowledge that has little application in the real world.[/40] [37]I share the view that we need a radical overhaul of how we teach, moving toward a more pragmatic approach that bridges the gap between the classroom and the workplace.[/37]
texts.C: The narrative that 'everyone needs a degree' has led to a significant skills gap in our economy. We have a surplus of graduates in humanities and social sciences who are underemployed, while technical trades go unfilled. [39]Unlike those who doubt the innovative power of the university, I would argue that academic freedom allows for the kind of 'blue-sky' research that corporations, with their focus on short-term profit, would never fund.[/39] This foundational research is what eventually leads to the technological breakthroughs of the future. [40]However, the idea that students are not prepared for work is an oversimplification; a university's job is to teach one how to learn, not to act as a vocational training center for specific companies.[/40] We must protect the university as a space for pure intellectual inquiry.
texts.D: The digital revolution has democratized knowledge, making the university's role as a gatekeeper of information obsolete. [38]I am convinced that within a generation, the traditional four-year residential degree will be a niche luxury for the wealthy, rather than the standard path for the many.[/38] Most students are now seeking 'just-in-time' learning—acquiring skills exactly when they need them for their careers. [37]I am less certain than some of my colleagues that these new forms of education can fully replicate the rigors of academic discipline, but the economic reality of debt-strapped graduates makes the current system unsustainable.[/37] The market is already shifting toward 'skill-based hiring,' where what you can actually do matters far more than where you went to school.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the necessity of reforming the current university curriculum to be more practical?
questions.explanation: B argues for a 'pragmatic approach' because the curriculum is 'outdated.' C, however, believes it is an 'oversimplification' to say students aren't prepared for work, arguing the university should NOT be a 'vocational training center.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding the long-term survival of the traditional campus-based degree?
questions.explanation: B believes the physical university is 'irreplaceable.' D is convinced that the traditional degree will soon become a 'niche luxury' and is essentially being replaced by 'just-in-time' learning.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's view on the importance of universities in driving long-term innovation?
questions.explanation: C argues that academic freedom allows for 'blue-sky research' that leads to future breakthroughs. While B focuses on the 'intellectual foundation,' B also rejects A's idea that universities are no longer relevant for innovation, supporting the intrinsic value of the academic model.

questions.question: Which expert has a similar view to B on whether universities are currently failing to prepare students for the practicalities of the job market?
questions.explanation: B says the curriculum is 'outdated' and lacks 'real world' application. A shares this sentiment, stating that the future lies in online certifications that meet the 'specific needs of the tech-driven economy,' implying the current model is no longer fit for purpose.
